quotes.text: Watch your thoughts.
Control your tongue.
Be pure and live with the pure.
Pure in word and deed and heart.
quotes.source: Questions of King Milinda (Book 4, ch. 4, sec. 43)

quotes.text: The higher life maketh he known, in all its purity and in all its perfectness.
To save countless beings, Not omitting even the least in his intention.
The birds and beasts and creeping things — ‘tis writ — Had sense of Buddha’s vast embracing love, And took the promise of his piteous speech.
quotes.source: Sir Edwin Arnold (Light of Asia, bk. 8)

quotes.text: On first awaking from my sleep, I should pray that every breathing thing may wake to saving wisdom, vast as the wide and boundless universe.
He cherished the feeling of affection for all beings as if they were his only son.
Closely as cause and effect are bound together, So do two loving hearts entwine and live — Such is the power of love to join in one.
Full of compassion for every living being.
That thou mayst know — What others will not — that i loved thee most because i loved so well all living souls.
quotes.source: Sir Edwin Arnold (Light of Asia, bk. 4)

quotes.text: There is no sweet companion like pure charity.
Ever exercising love towards the infirm.
Exceedingly loving towards the people, and whose hearts melt with pity.
Ever inspired by pity and love to men.
Good is restraint in all things.
Unselfish, true, and self-controlled.
The religious mendicant, wisely reflecting, is patient under cold and heat, under hunger and thirst, … under bodily suffering, under pains however sharp.
Though a man conquer a thousand thousand men in battle, a greater conqueror still is he who conquers himself.
Root out the love of self.
The man of honour should minister to his friends … by liberality, courtesy, benevolence, and by doing to them as he would be done by.
Practise the art of ‘giving up.’
quotes.note: I.e., putting away all desire and giving up ‘self.’

quotes.text: Speak not harshly to anybody.
May I speak kindly and softly to everyone I chance to meet.
quotes.source: Inscription in Temple of Nakhon Vat

quotes.text: Let him not, even though irritated, speak harsh words.
Offensive language is harsh even to the brutes.
Whatever word is humane… and lovely, reaching to the heart, … pleasing to the people, beloved of the people — such are the words he speaks.
Let him neither be led into falsehood, nor consciously do wicked things.
Let a man say that which is right, not that which is unrighteous, … that which is pleasing, not that which is unpleasing, … that which is true, not that which is false.
As he who loves life avoids poison, so let the sage avoid sinfulness.
He sees danger in even the least of those things he should avoid.
Sin easily develops.
May I never do, nor cause to be done, nor contemplate the doing of, even the most trivial sin!
To be firm and resolute as iron.
A Firm but loving heart.
Let not one who is asked for his pardon withhold it.
‘Tis wrong to conquer him who sues for mercy.
